2606.26298.title: Governing Actions, Not Agents: Institutional Attestation as a Governance Model for Autonomous AI Systems
2606.26298.paper_summary: Autonomous AI agents may begin to perform consequential, irreversible actions such as clinical prescribing and production software deployment. This paper observes that human institutions have governed powerful autonomous actors not by monitoring their reasoning but by requiring independently attested evidence at the point of consequential action. We formalise this institutional pattern as a computational governance model for AI agent systems. Under the proposed model, an agent retains full autonomy over planning and reasoning but holds no execution authority over designated high-risk actions. Execution is conditional on preconditions that are each independently attested by a separate authoritative source, cryptographically bound to a declared intent, and evaluated by a deterministic policy. Decisions are recorded in a tamper-evident log amenable to independent re-verification. We present a proof-of-concept implementation and illustrate the model with examples from software deployment and clinical prescribing.
2606.26298.paper_summary_zh: 自主 AI 代理可能開始執行有重大影響且不可逆的行動，例如臨床處方和生產軟體部署。本文觀察到，人類機構並不是通過監控其推理來管理強大的自主行為者，而是通過在有重大行動的時刻要求獨立證實的證據。我們將這種制度模式正式化為 AI 代理系統的計算治理模型。在所提出的模型下，代理在計劃和推理方面保持完全自主，但對指定的高風險行動沒有執行權限。執行條件取決於每個由獨立權威來源獨立證實的前提條件，這些前提條件以加密方式綁定於聲明的意圖，並由確定性政策進行評估。決策記錄在一個可防篡改的日誌中，便於獨立重新驗證。我們展示了一個概念驗證實現，並用軟體部署和臨床處方的例子來說明該模型。
2606.26298.author: Jakob Salfeld-Nebgen et.al.
